response:
Decision: **150 requests/day per key** (not the 1,000 the infra was built for), recorded in `DECISION.md`.

Rationale, briefly:
- The 30-day export shows even the busiest day averaged only ~106 req/key (peak: 2026-06-19, 4,770 req / 45 keys); typical days run ~95-105. A 1,000/day cap is ~10x that — it would never bind, so it can't do the one job a free-tier cap has: separating light users from heavy ones and giving heavy ones a reason to upgrade.
- "The metering pipeline already supports 1,000" is a sunk engineering cost, not a pricing rationale — it says the ceiling is safe to raise later, not where the cap should sit today.
- 150 sits ~40% above the highest per-key day observed, so no current free user should notice anything at launch, while still being tight enough to catch usage heavier than what's in this cohort (including any outlier key hidden inside a day's aggregate mean — the CSV only has daily totals, not per-key percentiles).

What would change it: raise it if free users start churning angry rather than upgrading once they hit the wall, or if per-key data (once available) shows the mean is skewed by a few outliers and the real median is well under 150; lower it if free-tier cost becomes a problem before conversions materialize. Either way, revisit after Pro has run 60-90 days, since this whole cap-to-conversion relationship is currently a guess.

--- recorded decision file DECISION.md ---
# Decision: free-tier daily request cap

Date: 2026-07-14
Decided by: Claude (delegated authority per founder's request)

## Decision
Set the free-tier cap at **150 requests/day per key**, not 1,000.

## Rationale

**The data rules out 1,000 outright.** Across the 30-day export, the busiest single
day still averaged only ~106 requests per active key (2026-06-19: 4,770 requests /
45 keys), and the typical day sits around 95-105. A 1,000/day cap is roughly 10x
the worst day on record. At that level the cap isn't a product decision at all —
it's a number nobody will ever reach, so it can't do the one job it exists to do,
which is separate light users from heavy ones and give the heavy ones a reason to
pay $12/mo.

**"We already built for 1,000" is a sunk cost, not a reason.** The metering
pipeline being sized and load-tested for 1,000/day is an engineering fact, not a
pricing fact. It tells us the ceiling is safe to raise later if needed; it says
nothing about where the cap should sit today. Shipping 1,000 because it's free to
build would be optimizing for "don't waste the sprint" over "make the business
work."

**150 is chosen, not just "the observed max."** It sits about 40% above the
highest per-key daily average seen in 30 days (106), so it will not surprise or
inconvenience any currently-typical free user at launch — nobody should see a
behavior change on day one. But it's low enough, relative to what the infra can
handle, to actually bind for usage that's heavier than what we've observed: new
signups whose habits differ from the current cohort, existing users who ramp up,
or any single key that's already an outlier hidden inside a day's aggregate
average (the CSV only has daily totals divided by active-key counts, not
per-key percentiles, so outliers above the mean are plausible and invisible in
this data).

**Rejected alternatives:**
- *1,000/day (Option A):* rejected — costs nothing to ship but also converts
  nobody; defeats the purpose of having a cap at billing launch.
- *Cap-then-revisit-later with no target (Option C, unscoped):* rejected as
  stated — "somewhere in between, decide later" isn't a decision, it's deferring
  the decision while calling it made. 150 *is* the point in between, chosen
  deliberately rather than left open.

## What would change this number
- **Raise it** if, once Pro is live, we see meaningful numbers of legitimate
  free users bumping the wall and churning away angry rather than upgrading —
  that would mean 150 is closer to "typical" than the aggregate data suggested
  and is punishing normal use, not just heavy use.
- **Raise it** if per-key usage logs (once available — the CSV only has daily
  aggregates) show the current mean is being pulled up by a few outliers and
  the real median free user is well under 150, in which case the cap could
  still be tightened toward that median instead.
- **Lower it** if free-tier costs (infra spend, support load) turn out to be a
  problem before enough users convert — 150 was picked to be gentle on launch,
  not to minimize free-tier cost.
- **Revisit on a fixed schedule regardless:** re-run this analysis after Pro
  has 60-90 days of real conversion data, since right now the entire cap-vs-
  conversion relationship is a guess with zero data behind it.
